1: A shadow: "Six days before the Passover, Jesus came to Bethany." The Passover countdown frames everything from this point on — the Lamb is approaching the feast of his own sacrifice. Bethany is the home of Lazarus, who has been raised from the dead; Jesus returns to the site of the greatest sign before entering Jerusalem for the last time. The Evangelist places the anointing narrative at the intersection of the Lazarus sign and the approaching Passover, so that the death and resurrection of Lazarus becomes the immediate context for understanding the death and resurrection of Jesus.
2: A revelation of God: Lazarus is "among those reclining at the table" — the one who was dead is now eating with Jesus. His presence at the table is itself a sign: the raised Lazarus sharing a meal with the resurrection-and-life is a preview of the eschatological banquet (Revelation 19:9) where all the raised will feast with the Lamb. Martha serves; the resurrection man reclines; the act of anointing is about to occur. The Evangelist arranges the scene so that every element anticipates what is coming.
3: A revelation of God: Mary's anointing of Jesus' feet with expensive nard and wiping them with her hair is an act of extraordinary devotion that the Evangelist explicitly links to his burial (v7). The anointing of a body was preparation for death; Mary performs it on the living Christ. She acts prophetically without knowing the full scope of her act. The fragrance filling the house is a detail the Evangelist preserves: the death of Jesus will fill the world with the fragrance of the knowledge of God (cf. 2 Corinthians 2:14–15).
4: A theme: Judas Iscariot's introduction — "who was later to betray him" — is the Evangelist's way of marking every subsequent statement of Judas as coming from one whose end is already known. The contrast between Mary's extravagant devotion and Judas's calculating objection is the contrast between two responses to Christ: costly, unreserved self-giving and self-interested calculation. Both will play out to their ends in the chapters that follow.
5: A revelation of God: the objection to extravagant devotion toward Christ — "Why wasn't this perfume sold and the money given to the poor?" — sounds reasonable and has the form of piety. But the Evangelist will expose its real motive (v6). The deeper principle Jesus defends (v7–8) is the priority of personal presence and devotion: the poor are always with you; the incarnate Son is not. The irreplaceable, unrepeatable presence of Christ in the world demands a response proportionate to who he is.
6: A revelation of God: the narrator's disclosure that Judas "used to help himself to what was put into" the money bag is not incidental detail but a revelation of the corruption that was present in the inner circle throughout the ministry. The enemy's work of undermining the mission did not begin at the Last Supper; it was present from early on (John 6:70–71). The Evangelist prepares the reader for the betrayal by showing that Judas's heart was already formed against Christ before the final decision is made.
7: A revelation of God: "It was intended that she should save this perfume for the day of my burial." Jesus explicitly connects Mary's act to his coming death. The anointing that should properly follow death is here performed before it — because the one being anointed knows what is coming and accepts it. He is the willing sacrifice who approaches his death with full foreknowledge. The act that might seem premature is precisely on time: six days before the Passover, the lamb is marked for the altar.
8: A revelation of God: "You will always have the poor among you, but you will not always have me" — the statement is not a dismissal of care for the poor but a claim about the uniqueness and finitude of the incarnate presence. Deuteronomy 15:11 ("there will always be poor people in the land") is cited as the premise; the conclusion is: this moment of incarnate presence is irreplaceable. The principle applies to the passion: no other moment of divine self-giving through death and resurrection exists. The response appropriate to Christ is proportionate to what he uniquely is and does.
9: A theme: the crowd comes to see Lazarus as well as Jesus — the raised man is himself a sign that draws people to the life-giver. The Evangelist shows the escalating impact of the Lazarus sign: it drives faith (John 11:45), triggers the Sanhedrin's decision (John 11:47–53), and continues to draw crowds toward Jesus. Lazarus's life is the Evangelist's ongoing testimony to what Christ does — a living sign that walks around Bethany.
10: A revelation of God: "the chief priests made plans to kill Lazarus as well." The decision to eliminate the evidence of the resurrection discloses the logic of unbelief at its most extreme: not "this evidence should change our minds" but "this evidence must be removed." The same logic governs all opposition to Christ in John — the signs are real, the authorities know they are real, and the response is not faith but intensified opposition. The resurrection sign cannot be undone; the attempt to undo it is the absurdity of trying to re-kill the raised.
11: A revelation of God: "many of the Jews were going over to Jesus and believing in him" because of Lazarus. The sign continues to produce faith even as the authorities plot against it. The Evangelist shows that the opposition of the powerful cannot stop the drawing power of the one who raises the dead — a principle that will govern the entire history of the church: persecution of witnesses does not stop witness, and attempts to silence the testimony of resurrection only underscore its power.
12: A shadow: the Triumphal Entry is the moment the Evangelist has been building toward since John 1:11 ("he came to his own"). The great crowd from the festival — Jews gathered for the Passover — go out to meet him. The OT pattern of the welcoming procession for the victorious king (Psalms 47:7–9; Psalms 68:24–27) is about to be enacted. But the king who comes is not the conquering military hero they expect; he comes on a donkey's colt (v14–15), the sign of the humble king Zechariah promised.
13: A direct fulfillment: "Hosanna! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Blessed is the king of Israel!" The crowd quotes Psalms 118:25–26 — the Hallel psalm sung at Passover, celebrating the one whom the Lord has made the cornerstone (Psalms 118:22). "Hosanna" (hōshia na — "save us, we pray") becomes the acclamation of the one who will save, though the crowd still imagines a political salvation. The king of Israel they are welcoming is the King of kings; the salvation they are asking for is deeper than they know.
14: A direct fulfillment: "Jesus found a young donkey and sat on it, as it is written." The Evangelist explicitly cites Scripture — the entry on a donkey is deliberate fulfillment of what was written. The choice of a donkey rather than a war horse (cf. Revelation 19:11) signals the kind of king Jesus is: the servant-king, the humble king, the king whose kingdom is not of this world (John 18:36). The fulfillment is precise and the Evangelist marks it as such.
15: A direct fulfillment: Zechariah 9:9 — "Do not be afraid, Daughter Zion; see, your king is coming, seated on a donkey's colt" — is fulfilled at the entry. Zechariah's vision of the king who arrives humble, riding on a donkey, who will speak peace to the nations (Zechariah 9:10), is the vision Christ enacts. The Evangelist quotes it to show that the Triumphal Entry is not a popular enthusiasm but a scripted fulfillment of prophetic vision. The crowd waves branches; the prophet sees the king approaching peace.
16: A revelation of God: "at first his disciples did not understand all this. Only after Jesus was glorified did they realize that these things had been written about him and that these things had been done to him." The post-resurrection retrospective is the Evangelist's epistemological principle stated explicitly: the Scripture that pointed to Christ was not fully understood until the resurrection made the referent clear. The Spirit would "remind them of everything" (John 14:26) — the fulfillment illuminates the prophecy. This is how biblical typology works: the type is recognized as type only when the antitype arrives.
17: A theme: the crowd that witnessed the raising of Lazarus keeps spreading the word. The testimony of the resurrection sign is self-propagating — those who saw it cannot stop talking about it. The Evangelist presents witness as the natural consequence of encounter with the sign of resurrection. The same dynamic will characterize the early church after the resurrection of Jesus: those who have seen cannot be silenced (Acts 4:20 — "we cannot help speaking about what we have seen and heard").
18: A revelation of God: the crowd goes to meet Jesus because of the resurrection sign, not yet because of who he is. The Evangelist has consistently distinguished sign-based response from the deeper faith that rests on Christ's word alone (John 4:50; John 4:48; John 2:23–25). The triumphal welcome is real but insufficient — it will evaporate by the week's end. The hosannas of Sunday become the "crucify him" of Friday, because the faith was grounded in expectation of a particular kind of king, not in recognition of who Jesus actually is.
19: A revelation of God: the Pharisees' complaint — "Look how the whole world has gone after him!" — is the Evangelist's irony operating at full force. They intend it as hyperbole of despair; the Evangelist means it as the truth: the whole world is what Jesus came for (John 3:16–17; John 4:42). The Pharisees' frustration is the inadvertent announcement that the mission is succeeding. The gathering of "the whole world" to Christ is the fulfillment of Isaiah's vision of the nations coming to the LORD's anointed.
20: A direct revelation: "Now there were some Greeks among those who went up to worship at the festival." The arrival of Greeks is the event that triggers Jesus' announcement that "the hour has come" (v23). The appearance of the first Gentile seekers in Jerusalem is the sign that the time of universal gathering has arrived. Isaiah 49:6 ("I will make you a light for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the ends of the earth") is about to be enacted — but through the cross. The Gentiles arrive; the hour of glorification (crucifixion) is announced.
21: A theme: "Sir, we would like to see Jesus." The Greeks' request is the most basic form of the faith the Gospel calls for — they want to see Jesus. Their approach through Philip (who has a Greek name, from Bethsaida near the Gentile regions) and Andrew (also a Greek name) reflects the gentle navigation of a boundary. The Evangelist frames their desire as the desire the entire book is designed to satisfy: the Gospel of John is written so that people may "see" Jesus in the Christological sense — believe, receive, and know.
22: A theme: the chain of witness — from the Greeks to Philip to Andrew to Jesus — is the pattern of NT mission. The desire to see Jesus is transmitted through human intermediaries who carry the request to the source. The Evangelist uses this narrative to model what will become the church's pattern: Gentiles reach toward Christ through believers who bring them to him. The chain from Greek seekers to Philip to Andrew to Jesus prefigures the chain from the apostles to the nations.
23: A direct revelation: "The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified." The Evangelist has been using "the hour" throughout the Gospel as the marker of the passion: "my hour has not yet come" (John 2:4; John 7:30; John 8:20). Now, in response to the arrival of Gentile seekers, the announcement is made: the hour is here. The glorification of the Son of Man is the cross — the moment the Evangelist has consistently framed as simultaneous death and exaltation (cf. John 3:14; John 8:28; John 12:32). The arrival of the Gentiles triggers the announcement because the cross is the event through which the Gentiles will be drawn to him (v32).
24: A shadow: "unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a single seed. But if it dies, it produces many seeds." The agricultural image of death producing life is the pattern of resurrection stated in the language of creation. Paul will use the same image in 1 Corinthians 15:36–38 to explain the resurrection body. Christ applies it to himself first: he is the grain that must die to produce the harvest of the Gentiles and all who believe. The pattern is embedded in creation — the seed's death is not failure but the precondition of abundance.
25: A revelation of God: "Anyone who loves their life will lose it, while anyone who hates their life in this world will keep it for eternal life." The paradox of losing to gain is the principle that governs Christ's own mission — he lays down his life and takes it up again (John 10:17–18). It is also the principle he extends to his followers. The one who clings to mortal life forfeits the life of the age to come; the one who surrenders mortal life in following Christ receives the indestructible life the Son possesses. The cross is not an exception to this principle; it is its supreme enactment.
26: A revelation of God: "where I am, my servant also will be." The promise of co-presence with Christ — extended to all who serve and follow him — is the eschatological promise that runs through the farewell discourses (John 14:3; John 17:24). The Father's honoring of those who serve the Son is the extension of the Father-Son relationship to include all who are united to the Son. The one who follows Christ into death follows him also into the life and honor that is the Father's response to the Son.
27: A revelation of God: "Now my soul is troubled, and what shall I say? Father, save me from this hour? No, it was for this very reason I came to this hour." The troubled soul is the Johannine equivalent of the Gethsemane prayer in the Synoptics (Matthew 26:38–42; Mark 14:34–36). The Evangelist compresses the anguish into a rhetorical question that is immediately answered by the Son himself: he came for this hour. The rejection of rescue and the embrace of the mission reflects the perfect alignment of the Son's will with the Father's even when the cost is extreme. The groaning of the Servant (Isaiah 53:10 — "the LORD's will to crush him") is accepted here.
28: A direct revelation: "Father, glorify your name!" — the prayer is answered immediately: "I have glorified it, and will glorify it again." The voice from heaven is the Father's testimony to the Son at the moment of the passion (cf. the baptism, Matthew 3:17, and the transfiguration, Matthew 17:5). The three divine attestations in Jesus' ministry — baptism, transfiguration, and here — are the Father's three-witness confirmation of the Son's identity and mission. The glorification has been accomplished (in the ministry) and will be accomplished again (in the cross and resurrection).
29: A revelation of God: the crowd hears the Father's voice and interprets it as thunder or an angel. The divine speech is real but inaccessible without faith. The same divine word that is testimony to the Son is noise to those who have not received him. The Evangelist's pattern is consistent: the revelation is objective and present; the reception depends on the orientation of the hearer. The heavenly voice at this moment is the Father's public ratification of what the Gospel has been narrating — but those who do not receive the Son cannot receive the Father's speech.
30: A revelation of God: "This voice was for your benefit, not mine." The Son's relationship with the Father does not require audible external confirmation — he knows the Father's will and speaks from direct communion. The voice was a concession to those present, an opportunity for them to hear the Father's testimony. Even this is the missionary posture of the Son: every act and word and divine attestation is oriented toward others' faith, not the Son's own need.
31: A direct revelation: "Now is the time for judgment on this world; now the prince of this world will be driven out." The cross is not merely a human execution but a cosmic event — the defeat of the ruler of the domain of death and sin. The "prince of this world" (ho archōn tou kosmou) is the power behind Judas (John 13:2), the one who entered Judas (John 13:27), and the one whose system Christ will expose as condemned (John 16:11). The crucifixion is the moment when the usurper is ejected and the rightful King is enthroned.
32: A direct revelation: "And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself." The lifting up is the crucifixion (v33) and simultaneously the glorification — the death is the moment of the Son's exaltation (cf. John 3:14; John 8:28). The drawing of "all people" (pantas) to the crucified Christ is the fulfillment of Isaiah 49:6 ("salvation to the ends of the earth") and the gathering of the scattered children of God (John 11:52). The cross is not defeat but the gravitational center of universal gathering. This is the Evangelist's theology of the atonement in one statement.
33: A revelation of God: the Evangelist clarifies that "lifted up" (hypsōthēnai) refers specifically to crucifixion. The ambiguity is deliberate — the same word denotes both the manner of execution (raised on a cross) and the nature of the event (exaltation, glorification). The Evangelist's theological claim is that these are inseparable: the cross is the exaltation; the shame is the glory; the death is the life. This is the paradox that the whole Gospel has been narrating since John 1:14.
34: A revelation of God: the crowd's question — "how can you say the Son of Man must be lifted up? Who is this Son of Man?" — reveals their messianic expectation: a Messiah who remains forever (Psalms 110:4; Daniel 7:14), not one who dies. They have received the teaching about the Son of Man's lifting up but cannot integrate it with their template for the Messiah. The question "Who is this Son of Man?" is the question the Gospel answers: he is the one who both dies and remains, who is both lifted up in death and exalted in resurrection.
35: A revelation of God: "Walk while you have the light, before darkness overtakes you." The light/darkness motif that has run through John from John 1:4–5 is here given its final urgency. The "little while longer" refers to the remaining hours of the public ministry before the arrest. The call to walk in the light is the call to respond to Christ while the opportunity exists. The darkness that is coming is both the literal night of the betrayal and the spiritual darkness that rejection of Christ produces.
36: A revelation of God: "Believe in the light while you have the light, so that you may become children of light." The invitation to believe in the light — who is Christ (John 1:9; John 8:12; John 9:5) — is the final public invitation in the Gospel before the farewell discourses. "Children of light" (huioi phōtos) is the identity of those who receive the light: they participate in the light's nature rather than merely benefiting from it. The Gospel of John's entire Christological program — light coming into darkness, darkness not overcoming it — comes to its public conclusion here as Jesus withdraws (v36b).
37: A revelation of God: "Even after Jesus had performed so many signs in their presence, they still would not believe in him." The summary of the public ministry is the Evangelist's most direct statement of the rejection that John 1:11 announced: "He came to his own, and his own did not receive him." The signs were real; the rejection is real; the tragedy is real. The Evangelist is not minimizing the failure but explaining it: what follows in vv38–41 shows that the Isaiah passages anticipated this exact rejection.
38: A direct fulfillment: "This was to fulfill the word of Isaiah the prophet: 'Lord, who has believed our message and to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?'" The Evangelist quotes Isaiah 53:1 — the opening of the Servant Song that describes the Servant's rejection — as the scriptural anticipation of Israel's failure to receive Christ. The arm of the Lord (the power and saving action of God) has been revealed in the signs; it has not been received. Isaiah's question was already the question of a generation that refused to see the Servant's significance; the Evangelist applies it to the generation that refused to see Jesus' significance.
39: A revelation of God: "for this reason they could not believe" — not merely "did not believe" but "could not." The Evangelist introduces the language of moral-judicial inability: the rejection has hardened into a state in which belief is no longer accessible. This is the pattern Paul describes in Romans 9–11 (the hardening of part of Israel) and that Jesus applied to the Pharisees (John 8:43 — "you are unable to hear my word").
40: A direct fulfillment: the Evangelist quotes Isaiah 6:9–10 — "He has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts, so they can neither see with their eyes, nor understand with their hearts, nor turn — and I would heal them." The Isaiah 6 commission was the hardening judgment pronounced on Israel at the beginning of Isaiah's ministry. The Evangelist applies it to the end of Jesus' public ministry: the same pattern of hardening-under-revelation has recurred. God does not arbitrarily blind; the blinding is the judicial consequence of sustained rejection of what is clearly revealed.
41: A direct revelation: "Isaiah said this because he saw Jesus' glory and spoke about him." The Evangelist makes the most explicit Christological claim about an OT text in the Gospel: Isaiah's vision in chapter 6 — the LORD seated on the throne, high and lifted up, the seraphim crying "Holy, holy, holy" — was a vision of the glory of the pre-incarnate Son. The one who is "the image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15), through whom all things were made (John 1:3), was the one Isaiah saw. The "LORD of hosts" of Isaiah 6:3 is identified with the pre-incarnate Christ.
42: A revelation of God: "many even among the leaders believed in him" — the faith of secret disciples (Joseph of Arimathea, Nicodemus at night) represents the fragment of Israel's leadership that does receive Jesus. Their secrecy is exposed by the Evangelist as a failure of courage (fear of the synagogue), but their faith is not dismissed. The Evangelist preserves them as evidence that the rejection of Jesus was not total and that the judgment on unbelief is proportionate to the knowing rejection, not a blanket condemnation of everyone in Israel.
43: A revelation of God: "they loved human praise more than praise from God." The critique Jesus made in 5:44 is now stated by the Evangelist as the structural explanation of why the secret disciples remained secret. The honor-from-peers system that Jesus identified as the obstacle to faith (John 5:44) is the trap in which many who believe remain caught. Faith that cannot be confessed is not yet the faith the Gospel calls for — "with the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved" (Romans 10:10).
44: A direct revelation: "Whoever believes in me does not believe in me only, but in the one who sent me." The inseparability of the Son from the Father — stated throughout John (John 10:30; John 14:9–10) — is here applied to the act of faith. To believe in Jesus is to believe in the Father who sent him; to reject Jesus is to reject the Father. The sending relationship that pervades John (the Father sends the Son) means that the Son is not an independent figure but the presence and action of the Father in the world.
45: A direct revelation: "The one who looks at me is seeing the one who sent me." The vision of Christ is the vision of the Father — not because they are identical persons but because the Son is the image and revelation of the Father (John 14:9 — "Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father"). The Prologue's claim (John 1:18 — "the one and only Son, who is himself God and is in closest relationship with the Father, has made him known") is here restated in terms of vision: the incarnate Son is the definitive disclosure of who God is.
46: A direct revelation: "I have come into the world as a light, so that no one who believes in me should stay in darkness." The programmatic statement of John 1:9 ("the true light that gives light to everyone was coming into the world") finds its closing Christological statement here: the light has come; belief in him is the exit from darkness. The purpose of the incarnation — light into darkness so that darkness is no longer inevitable for those who receive the light — is stated with maximum directness in this final public declaration.
47: A revelation of God: "I did not come to judge the world, but to save the world." The mission statement of 3:17 is repeated in the closing of the public ministry. The first coming is salvation; the judgment comes at the last day through the word (v48). The compassionate intent of the incarnation — rescue, not condemnation — is maintained to the end of the public ministry. Those who hear and do not keep are not yet judged; they are still within the scope of the salvific offer.
48: A direct revelation: "the very words I have spoken will condemn them at the last day." The words of Jesus — which are the Father's commands (v49) — are the standard of the last judgment. This is the inseparability of Christ and Scripture: the word of Jesus is not only grace but, when rejected, is the measure of rejection. Those who have heard and refused have their condemnation already in the words they have refused. The last day does not produce new evidence; it reveals what the response to Christ's word has always been.
49: A revelation of God: "I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken." The complete authority of Christ's teaching is grounded in its source: every word the Son has spoken is the Father's command. This is the Christological basis for treating Jesus' teachings as Scripture: he speaks not as a prophet who receives occasional divine messages but as the Son who speaks only what he has heard from the Father (John 8:26, 40). The word of Christ is the word of God.
50: A revelation of God: "his command leads to eternal life. So whatever I say is just what the Father has told me to say." The final words of the public ministry in John affirm the soteriological and revelatory sufficiency of Christ's teaching. Everything the Son has spoken — the seven signs, the discourses, the dialogues, the "I am" statements — is the Father's own communication, and its content is eternal life. The public ministry closes with a statement of absolute Christological authority: every syllable from the Son is from the Father, and the destination of it all is life.
